description:
## Finding

When a signing key's actual type/curve/size does not match what the caller/URI expects, the two hardware-backed backends return different error variants:

**secretx-tpm2** uses `SecretError::AlgorithmMismatch`:
```rust
// validate_key_type(), lib.rs:543
return Err(SecretError::AlgorithmMismatch {
    expected: "ecdsa-p256",
    actual: format!("TPM key uses curve {:?}", parameters.ecc_curve()),
});
```

**secretx-pkcs11** uses `SecretError::Backend` (via `pkcs11_err`):
```rust
// detect_ec_algorithm(), lib.rs:364
Err(pkcs11_err(format!(
    "unsupported EC curve (CKA_EC_PARAMS = {:02x?}); only P-256 is supported",
    params
)))
```

The core `AlgorithmMismatch` variant was designed for this exact scenario (lib.rs:1076–1084 doc: "the backend's key algorithm does not match what the caller expected"). PKCS11 is not using it.

**secretx-aws-kms** defers entirely to server-side enforcement — a mismatch produces an opaque `InvalidKeyUsageException` that is classified as `Backend`.

## Impact

A caller matching on `SecretError::AlgorithmMismatch` to produce a user-friendly "you configured the wrong key type" message will:
- Correctly catch tpm2 mismatches
- Miss pkcs11 mismatches (they arrive as generic `Backend` errors with an opaque message)
- Miss aws-kms mismatches (same)

The `AlgorithmMismatch` variant exists precisely to make this matchable. PKCS11's `detect_ec_algorithm` and `detect_rsa_algorithm` should use it.

## Locations

- `/home/mark/PROJECT/crate-secretx/secretx-tpm2/src/lib.rs` lines 533–568 (`validate_key_type`)
- `/home/mark/PROJECT/crate-secretx/secretx-pkcs11/src/lib.rs` lines 323–369 (`detect_rsa_algorithm`, `detect_ec_algorithm`, line 473)
- `/home/mark/PROJECT/crate-secretx/secretx-core/src/lib.rs` lines 1076–1084 (`AlgorithmMismatch` definition)
title: Key-type mismatch: TPM2 uses AlgorithmMismatch, PKCS11 uses Backend
